key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying makes NiCrFeBSiC coatings thicker, denser, and more wear resistant.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a simple process-control lever for improving coating quality and durability in thermal spray applications.
method: Air plasma sprayed self-fluxing NiCrFeBSiC metal-ceramic coatings on a substrate.
Compared two argon flow rates: 1600 and 1800 L/h.
Characterized thickness, porosity, microhardness, modulus, friction, and adhesion/scratch behavior.
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness roughly doubled, porosity dropped by about half, and friction coefficient decreased to about 0.107 while adhesion remained strong.
watch_item: The abstract compares only two process settings, so broader optimization and long-term wear validation are still needed.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
